appSubtitle: Presidential AI Challenge · Zoom Interview Prep

relevance.q: There's a big national goal to teach all kids about AI. How does your project connect to that goal?
relevance.a: Our project connects directly to the President's Executive Order on AI Education from April 2025, called Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth. That order says we need to reach 25 to 30 million K-12 students across America. Our 3-person team built AI Glitch Buster to teach 4 AI safety topics through 12 videos and 240 quiz questions, hosted free on GitHub Pages. During testing we reached 121 users across 8 countries with 715 total interactions, and 90 percent said they learned something new. The problem is bigger than our local school.
keys: April 2025 Executive Order targets 25–30 million K-12 students
Free app: 12 videos + 240 questions across 4 AI safety topics
Reached 121 users in 8 countries; 90% reported learning something new

relevance.q: Why is it important for elementary school kids to learn about AI safety right now?
relevance.a: Elementary school is when habits form. A 3rd grader who learns AI can hallucinate — making up facts — will remember that at age 25. The April 2025 Executive Order calls for starting AI literacy across all K-12 grades. K-5 students are already using chatbots for homework but almost none have had even 1 lesson on AI risks. Our app fills that gap with 12 short videos and 240 quiz questions designed for grades K through 5. In testing, 90 percent of users gained new knowledge after just 1 session — proving young kids can learn these concepts when explained correctly.
keys: K-5 habits last a lifetime — strongest window for AI literacy
April 2025 Executive Order calls for AI education starting in K-5
90% of app users gained new AI safety knowledge in a single session

relevance.q: Can you name the specific government policy your project aligns with?
relevance.a: The specific policy is the Executive Order on AI Education signed in April 2025, titled Advancing Artificial Intelligence Education for American Youth. It has 3 main goals: teach AI literacy, promote responsible AI use, and prepare students to compete in an AI world. Our project addresses all 3. For literacy: 12 animated videos and 240 quiz questions covering 4 AI risks. For responsible use: our custom classifier trained on 360 examples has a human-in-the-loop feedback system. For competitiveness: we built a real AI tool from scratch. Deployed free on GitHub Pages so any of America's roughly 130,000 elementary schools can access it.
keys: Matches all 3 goals of the April 2025 AI Education Executive Order
12 videos + 240 questions for literacy; 360-example classifier for responsible use
Free deployment accessible to all 130,000+ U.S. elementary schools at zero cost

relevance.q: Your app reached users in 8 countries. What does that tell us about why AI safety education matters?
relevance.a: It tells us AI safety is a global problem, not just an American one. Our 3-person team in Portland built a free app and within our testing period had 121 users from 8 countries generate 715 total interactions with zero paid advertising. In America specifically, the April 2025 Executive Order highlights that 25 to 30 million K-12 students need this training. If a small elementary school team can reach 8 countries with 0 marketing dollars, imagine what a nationally supported program could do. Our project proves the concept works and that kids can lead the way.
keys: 121 users across 8 countries with 715 interactions — zero paid marketing
April 2025 Executive Order: 25–30 million U.S. K-12 students need AI literacy training
Organic global reach with zero ad budget proves content drives engagement

relevance.q: How does your project help America stay safe as AI becomes more powerful?
relevance.a: The biggest risk is blind trust — believing everything AI says without checking. Our app fights that with 240 quiz questions across 4 categories: bias, hallucination, privacy, and bad habits. Each question forces critical thinking. We also built a custom AI classifier trained on 360 examples that detects those 4 problem types in real sentences, so kids can test actual chatbot answers and see the classifier flag problems. In testing, 90 percent of 121 users across 8 countries gained new knowledge. If those habits stay with them, America's future workforce will question AI rather than blindly trust it — starting from a foundation built in K-5.
keys: 240 quiz questions across 4 risk categories train critical thinking habits
360-example classifier lets kids test real chatbot sentences for AI problems
90% of 121 users gained new knowledge — measurable impact on future AI behavior

relevance.q: If the President asked you in 30 seconds why your project matters, what would you say?
relevance.a: I would say: right now, 25 to 30 million K-12 students in America are using AI tools without any safety training. Your April 2025 Executive Order says we need to fix that. We are 3 elementary students from Portland who spent 12 weeks building a free app called AI Glitch Buster. It teaches 4 AI safety topics through 12 videos and 240 quiz questions. We also built a custom AI classifier trained on 360 examples so kids can see AI safety in action. We tested it with 121 users across 8 countries and 90 percent learned something new. Project number 20260094 — built by kids in 5th grade.
keys: 25–30 million K-12 students currently have no AI safety training
3 students, 12 weeks: 12 videos, 240 questions, 360-example classifier — all free
121 users in 8 countries, 90% learned something new — proven measurable impact

relevance.q: How does your project show that young people can help solve big national problems?
relevance.a: Our project is proof that age does not limit impact. We are 3 elementary school students — Aariv, Madesh, and Mishika — and in 12 weeks starting October 26 we built a working website with 12 videos, a 240-question quiz, and a custom AI text classifier trained on 360 examples. We tried 3 commercial platforms that failed, so we coded our own tool from scratch using Claude. We tested at a science fair with 30 to 40 visitors and collected feedback from 121 users across 8 countries. The national goal is to teach 25 to 30 million kids about AI safety — having kids lead that effort makes it more authentic because kids listen to other kids.
keys: 3 elementary students built 12 videos, 240 questions, 360-example classifier in 12 weeks
Tested with 30–40 at science fair; reached 121 users in 8 countries total
Kid-led projects reach K-5 peers more authentically than adult-created tools

technical.q: Can you walk me through how your AI text classifier works and what it does?
technical.a: Our AI text classifier reads a sentence and decides which of 4 categories it belongs to: bias, hallucination, privacy, or bad habits. To train it we fed it 360 example sentences — 30 examples per category per team member, with 3 team members and 4 categories. We started with just 2 examples per category and the classifier kept making wrong guesses on new sentences. After experimenting at 5, 10, and 20 examples per category we found that 20 was the minimum for reliable results. The classifier also has a human-in-the-loop feedback loop: when it makes a mistake, the user clicks to correct it and the tool learns from that correction. We built the entire training interface ourselves using Claude, because the 3 platforms we tried — Liner AI, Machine Learning for Kids, and Base44 — all had critical limitations.
keys: 360 training examples: 3 members × 4 topics × 30 examples each
Tested at 2, 5, 10, 20 examples — minimum 20 per category needed for reliability
Human-in-the-loop: user corrections stored and used to improve future classifications

technical.q: What tools did your team use to build the project and why did you choose them?
technical.a: We used 7 main tools. Claude was our primary coding partner for all website and classifier code. ChatGPT and Google Gemini helped us research 4 AI safety topics and write educational content. Perplexity helped us create better prompts. Adobe Express let us animate ourselves as teachers for all 12 videos. YouTube hosted those 12 videos embedded in our site with iframes. GitHub Pages hosts our website free — our coach helped since we are all under 13. Google Docs and Grammarly kept our 3-person team organized. The total cost of hosting and distribution was zero dollars, which matters because our target audience is 25 to 30 million K-12 students who need free access.
keys: 7 tools: Claude (code), ChatGPT/Gemini (research), Perplexity (prompts), Adobe Express (12 videos), YouTube, GitHub Pages, Google Docs
Claude wrote and debugged all code for the website and the 360-example AI classifier
GitHub Pages: zero-cost hosting for a tool targeting 25–30 million students

technical.q: Your quiz has 240 questions. How did you design and organize all of them?
technical.a: We organized 240 questions into 4 topics with 60 each: bias, hallucination, privacy, and bad habits. We started the quiz with only 5 questions per topic — 20 total — and expanded to 60 per topic during Phase 3 in late November, a 12-times increase. We discovered a critical bug at our December science fair: the correct answer was always in position 1. Visitors noticed and clicked option 1 without reading. We fixed this by randomizing the correct answer position across all 240 questions. The quiz also awards a Glitch Buster badge every 5 correct answers in a row. After all fixes, 90 percent of our 121 total users reported gaining new AI safety knowledge.
keys: 240 questions = 4 topics × 60; grew 12× from original 20 questions during Phase 3
Critical bug: answer always in position 1 — fixed by randomizing all 240 questions
Badge every 5 correct; post-fix: 90% of 121 users reported learning something new

technical.q: How did you make sure the app works on different devices like phones and laptops?
technical.a: We tested on 4 specific device types: Chromebooks, Windows laptops, iPhones, and Android phones — chosen because they cover the most common devices in American K-5 classrooms. During our December science fair with 30 to 40 visitors, we discovered that quiz text was getting cut off on phone screens and buttons were too small to tap. We fixed font sizes, button dimensions, and padding for smaller screens. We also verified that all 12 YouTube videos loaded correctly on all 4 device types via iframes. The final version passed testing on all 4 device types, so whether a student has a school Chromebook or a family iPhone, the full 240-question quiz and 12 videos are accessible.
keys: Tested on 4 device types: Chromebooks, Windows, iPhones, Android
Science fair (30–40 visitors) revealed phone layout bugs — fixed before final release
All 12 YouTube videos verified loading correctly on all 4 device types

technical.q: You tried 3 platforms before building your own tool. What went wrong with each one?
technical.a: The 3 platforms were Liner AI, Machine Learning for Kids, and Base44. Liner AI hit a capacity limit — we needed at least 20 examples per category across 4 categories, a minimum of 80 examples total, and Liner AI could not handle that. Machine Learning for Kids is designed for students but had no way to add the human-in-the-loop feedback feature we needed, where user corrections teach the model. Base44 got us the furthest but ran out of credits before we could finish loading all 360 training examples. After 3 failures, we used Claude to code our own custom tool from scratch with exactly 4 features: category definition, example storage, a 20-example minimum threshold, and user feedback correction.
keys: 3 platforms failed: Liner AI (capacity under 80 examples), ML for Kids (no customization), Base44 (ran out of credits before 360 examples)
Required minimum 80 examples across 4 categories — none of the 3 platforms handled it
Custom tool built from scratch using Claude with 4 specific features and 360-example capacity

technical.q: Can you explain synthetic data and why your team used it for training the classifier?
technical.a: Synthetic data means examples we created ourselves rather than collecting from real people. We used it because gathering 360 real examples of people accidentally sharing private info or believing AI hallucinations would be nearly impossible — and would raise serious privacy concerns for a tool designed for kids under 13. Instead, we used ChatGPT to help write realistic examples of what a biased, hallucinating, or privacy-violating AI response might look like across all 4 categories. Each of our 3 team members was responsible for 30 examples per topic — 120 per person, 360 total. Then every single one of the 360 examples was reviewed and validated by our team by hand before being used to train the classifier.
keys: 360 synthetic examples: 3 members × 4 topics × 30 examples = 360 total
All 360 generated by ChatGPT then validated by hand by the 3-person team
Synthetic approach avoids privacy issues — essential for a children's K-5 educational tool

technical.q: How did the Large Prompt Glitch happen and what exactly did you do to fix it?
technical.a: The Large Prompt Glitch happened in Phase 3, late November. We gave Claude 1 giant instruction describing the entire website — navigation, quiz logic, video embedding, and badge system all in 1 message. The code came back tangled and full of bugs; fixing 1 thing broke something else. The fix was modular coding — build 1 small component per session. We rebuilt the app in 4 separate sessions: navigation bar first, then quiz logic, then video iframes, then badge system. This fixed all the bugs and had an important side effect: we could now expand the quiz module independently. We grew from 5 questions per topic to 60 per topic — from 20 total questions to 240 — a 12-times increase, without touching the other 3 modules.
keys: Large Prompt Glitch: 1 giant Claude prompt produced tangled unfixable code
Fix: rebuilt app as 4 separate modules — navigation, quiz, video, badges
Modular fix enabled quiz growth from 20 to 240 questions (12×) without breaking other modules

process.q: Can you walk me through the 4 steps your team followed to build this project?
process.a: We started October 26 and followed 4 steps. Step 1, late October through early November — about 2 weeks — was planning and research. Each of our 3 team members researched 1 topic and combined notes in Google Docs. We also interviewed parents and teachers. Step 2, mid-November — about 1 week — was designing the user journey on paper before writing any code. Step 3, late November — about 2 weeks — was building. We fixed the Large Prompt Glitch, expanded the quiz from 5 to 60 questions per topic growing from 20 to 240 total, and trained the classifier on 360 examples. Step 4, December — about 2 to 3 weeks — was testing with 30 to 40 visitors at the science fair and making improvements. From October 26 to our January 20, 2026 submission is approximately 12 weeks total.
keys: 4 steps over 12 weeks: research (2 wks), paper design (1 wk), build (2 wks), test (2–3 wks)
Quiz grew from 20 to 240 questions (12×); classifier grew to 360 examples — both in Step 3
Science fair in Step 4: 30–40 real users gave feedback that improved the final app

process.q: What was the biggest technical problem you ran into and how did you solve it?
process.a: We had 2 big problems. Problem 1 was the Large Prompt Glitch in late November. We gave Claude 1 giant instruction for the whole app and the code was a tangled mess. We fixed it by breaking work into 4 modules — navigation, quiz, videos, badges — built in separate sessions. That fix alone let us grow the quiz from 20 total questions to 240, a 12-times increase. Problem 2 was our AI classifier. We started with 2 training examples per category and it kept failing on new sentences. We tested at 5, 10, and 20 examples and found 20 was the minimum for reliability. So we wrote 360 total examples — 30 per topic per team member. Both problems taught the same lesson: break it into smaller pieces and add more data.
keys: Large Prompt Glitch fixed by splitting into 4 separate code modules
Classifier needed minimum 20 examples per category — started at 2 and failed repeatedly
Fixing both problems: quiz grew from 20 to 240; classifier grew to 360 examples

process.q: You drew your app design on paper before coding. Why did that step matter?
process.a: Drawing on paper in mid-November — our 1-week Step 2 — saved us from wasting days of coding work. We mapped a user journey: every screen and how you move between them across all 4 topics. This took 1 week but caught a serious problem: we had forgotten to add a back button from the quiz to the home screen. Fixing that on paper took 30 seconds. Fixing it in code after building everything would have taken hours. Real software engineers call this wireframing. It also helped our 3-person team agree on exactly what we were building before anyone wrote a line of code. The user journey covered 6 screen types — home, 4 topic tiles, video page, quiz, badge page, and AI classifier — all designed on paper first.
keys: 1 week of paper design in mid-November caught missing navigation before any code was written
User journey covered 6 screen types across 4 topics — all sketched before coding
Prevented hours of rework — critical for a 3-person elementary team with a 12-week deadline

process.q: How did you test your project with real users and what changed because of their feedback?
process.a: We ran 3 testing rounds. Round 1 was internal: all 3 team members used the app daily and logged bugs. Round 2 was our December science fair: 30 to 40 visitors tested the live app. They told us quiz questions were too long and buttons were too small on phones. Round 3 was a Google Form survey shared on social media, reaching 121 users across 8 countries with 715 total interactions and 10 written feedback responses. The most important fix was a bug where the correct answer was always option 1 — we randomized positions across all 240 questions. We also shortened quiz text and increased button sizes. After those changes, 90 percent of users reported gaining new AI safety knowledge.
keys: 3 rounds: internal team, science fair (30–40 visitors), online survey (121 users, 8 countries)
Critical bug fixed: correct answer always option 1 — randomized across all 240 questions
Post-fix: 90% of 121 users reported learning something new; 715 total interactions logged

process.q: You interviewed parents and teachers before building anything. What did you learn from those interviews?
process.a: In Phase 1 — late October through early November — we interviewed parents and teachers to find out what worried them most about kids using AI. Parents were most concerned about privacy: kids sharing personal info with chatbots. Teachers were most concerned about hallucination: students submitting AI-invented facts as real homework. Both groups said they had 0 good tools to teach K-5 kids about these risks. Before the interviews we had only planned to cover 2 topics. After hearing from parents and teachers we expanded to all 4: bias, hallucination, privacy, and bad habits. That decision doubled our content from a planned 120 quiz questions to 240 final questions and directly led to adding all 12 videos.
keys: Interviews revealed 0 existing tools for K-5 AI safety education
Feedback expanded topics from 2 to 4, doubling quiz content from 120 to 240 questions
Parent feedback drove video decision — 12 animated videos added as a direct result

process.q: How did your 3-person team divide the 12 weeks of work?
process.a: We divided work by topic and role. For content, each of our 3 team members — Aariv, Madesh, and Mishika — took primary responsibility for 1 topic's video script and research. For the 360 classifier training examples, we each wrote 30 examples per topic across all 4 topics — 120 examples per person. For coding, all 3 of us contributed prompts and testing, rotating who submitted each module to Claude. For testing, all 3 of us used the app daily during Phases 3 and 4. At the December science fair all 3 of us presented to the 30 to 40 visitors and collected feedback. Dividing the 360 examples equally kept the workload balanced over the 12-week period.
keys: 360 examples divided equally: 120 per person (30 per topic × 4 topics)
All 3 presented at December science fair to 30–40 visitors
Google Docs kept a 3-person team aligned across all 12 weeks and 4 development steps

process.q: Your project started October 26 and was submitted January 20. Walk me through what happened each phase.
process.a: October 26 to November 10 — about 2 weeks — was Phase 1: brainstorming, researching all 4 topics using ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity, and interviewing parents and teachers. November 10 to November 17 — 1 week — was Phase 2: drawing the user journey on paper for all 4 topics before writing any code. November 17 to November 30 — 2 weeks — was Phase 3: hitting and fixing the Large Prompt Glitch, growing the quiz from 20 to 240 questions, and writing all 360 classifier training examples. December — 2 to 3 weeks — was Phase 4: testing at the science fair with 30 to 40 visitors, fixing the answer-position bug across all 240 questions, and polishing the design. January 1 to January 20 was final polishing and submitting Project 20260094.
keys: October 26 to November 10: research phase (2 weeks)
November 17 to November 30: build phase — quiz grew from 20 to 240; classifier to 360 examples
December to January 20: test, fix 240-question bug, polish, submit Project 20260094

ai_use.q: You built your own AI tool. How did you train it and how did you check if it was working?
ai_use.a: We trained our text classifier using 360 example sentences across 4 categories: bias, hallucination, privacy, and bad habits. Each of our 3 team members wrote 30 examples per topic — 120 per person, 360 total. We used ChatGPT to help generate synthetic examples, then validated all 360 by hand. To check accuracy, we tested the classifier with new sentences it had never seen during training. We started with 2 examples per category — accuracy was terrible. We tested at 5, 10, and 20 examples per category and found 20 was the minimum threshold for reliable classification. We also presented the live classifier at our December science fair, where 30 to 40 visitors typed in their own sentences and told us when it guessed wrong.
keys: 360 training examples: 3 members × 4 topics × 30 examples, all validated by hand
Tested at 2, 5, 10, 20 examples per category — minimum 20 needed for reliability
Science fair: 30–40 visitors typed real sentences to test live classifier accuracy

ai_use.q: How did you use AI tools responsibly while building this project?
ai_use.a: We used 4 AI tools — Claude, ChatGPT, Google Gemini, and Perplexity — with clear rules for each. Claude wrote and debugged all code, but we reviewed every output, tested it ourselves, and told Claude what was wrong — keeping us in charge at all times. ChatGPT and Gemini helped us research our 4 topics, but we rewrote all content in our own words. Perplexity helped us write better prompts. Gemini was used only for grammar and spelling cleanup in the final submission — all content was written by our 3-person team first. We included a full AI Use Note in Project 20260094 disclosing exactly which tool did what. We also validated all 360 classifier training examples by hand even though ChatGPT helped generate them.
keys: 4 AI tools, each with a defined role disclosed in the submitted AI Use Note
All 360 AI-generated training examples validated by hand by the 3-person team
0 unreviewed AI output published — all code and content reviewed before going live

ai_use.q: You got data from 121 users. What did the numbers tell you and what did you change?
ai_use.a: We collected data from a Google Form survey and website analytics. Across 121 users in 8 countries, we recorded 715 total interactions — an average of 5.9 interactions per user, showing most people explored multiple topics in 1 visit. Before using the app, only a small number of users felt confident about AI safety. After using it, 90 percent reported gaining valuable knowledge. We received 10 written feedback responses. The most critical finding was about the answer-position bug: the correct answer was always option 1. We fixed this by randomizing positions across all 240 questions. We also shortened quiz questions with low completion rates and increased button sizes on mobile.
keys: 121 users × 715 interactions = 5.9 interactions per user — high multi-topic engagement
90% reported learning something new — up from small minority before app improvements
10 written responses led to fixing answer randomization across all 240 questions

ai_use.q: What does human-in-the-loop mean and why did you build that into your AI classifier?
ai_use.a: Human-in-the-loop means a person is always part of the AI's decision process. In our classifier it works like this: the tool reads a sentence and guesses 1 of 4 categories. If it guesses wrong, the user clicks a correction button. That correction is saved and used to improve future guesses. We built this because it matches the core lesson we teach: AI should never operate without human oversight. It is also practical — our classifier starts with 360 training examples, which is a small dataset. Every user correction adds to that dataset and makes the model smarter over time. We did not just talk about responsible AI in our 12 videos and 240 questions — we built it as a technical feature inside our own AI tool.
keys: Human-in-the-loop: corrections saved and used to improve future classifications
Classifier grows smarter with every user correction beyond its initial 360 examples
Responsible AI built as a technical feature — not just taught in 12 videos but coded in the tool

ai_use.q: How did you validate that your AI classifier was actually accurate?
ai_use.a: We used 3 validation methods. First, after every training round we tested the classifier with at least 10 unseen sentences per category — sentences it had never seen during training — and manually verified each guess. Second, we presented the live classifier at our December science fair to 30 to 40 visitors who typed their own real chatbot sentences. We logged every wrong guess. Third, we kept a hold-out set of about 10 percent of our 360 examples — around 36 sentences — that were never used in training, only in evaluation. We found that below 20 training examples per category, accuracy was unreliable on unseen sentences. Above 20 examples — where our final set of 360 examples sits — performance improved significantly.
keys: 3 validation methods: 10 unseen sentences per category, 30–40 science fair testers, ~36-sentence hold-out set
Key threshold: below 20 examples per category = unreliable; 360 total examples = reliable
Human-in-the-loop means classifier accuracy improves beyond initial 360 examples over time

ai_use.q: Claude was your main coding partner. Can you give one specific example of a problem Claude helped you solve?
ai_use.a: The best example is the Large Prompt Glitch in Phase 3. We gave Claude 1 giant instruction in late November describing the entire app — navigation, quiz, 60-question pools, video iframes, and badge system all at once. The code came back buggy and tangled. Claude explained why: large prompts generate code with conflicting dependencies that are nearly impossible to debug. Claude recommended building in 4 separate modules. We rebuilt in 4 separate Claude sessions: navigation bar first, then quiz logic, then video embedding, then badge system. This eliminated all bugs. More importantly, it let us expand the quiz module independently — we grew from 5 questions per topic to 60 per topic, from 20 total to 240, a 12-times increase, without touching the other 3 modules.
keys: Large Prompt Glitch: 1 giant instruction produced tangled debugging-proof code
Claude taught modular coding — rebuilt as 4 separate sessions: navigation, quiz, video, badges
Modular fix enabled 12× quiz growth (20 to 240) without breaking the other 3 modules

ai_use.q: Your app reached 715 interactions across 121 users. How did you collect that data and what did it prove?
ai_use.a: We collected data through 2 channels. First, Google Analytics embedded in our GitHub Pages site tracked page views, session time, and which of the 4 topics got the most clicks. Second, a Google Form survey asked users how confident they felt about AI safety before and after the app, and what features to add. We shared the form through parents on social media at zero cost. The result: 121 users from 8 countries, 715 interactions, 5.9 interactions per user on average, and 10 written responses. The data proved 3 things: the problem is global with 8 countries reached and no paid promotion; engagement is high with nearly 6 interactions per visit; and the content works with 90 percent of survey respondents reporting new AI safety knowledge in just 1 session.
keys: Data from 2 sources: Google Analytics (behavior) + Google Form (self-reported learning)
715 interactions across 121 users = 5.9 per user — users explore multiple topics per visit
3 proven outcomes: global reach (8 countries), high engagement, 90% knowledge gain — zero ad spend

originality.q: What makes your project different from other AI safety resources that already exist?
originality.a: Almost all AI safety resources are written for adults or computer science researchers. We made 3 specific choices that set ours apart. First, reading level: we use soccer teams and superheroes to explain bias and keep every video to about 2 minutes. Second, creator identity: this was built by 3 elementary school students — not a tech company — which makes it authentic for K-5 kids. Third, technical depth: we built a custom text classifier trained on 360 examples with a human-in-the-loop system — real machine learning, not a pre-built tool. In our testing, 90 percent of 121 users said they learned something new, and we reached 8 countries with zero marketing dollars.
keys: 3 differentiators: K-5 language level, student creators, 360-example custom-built classifier
90% of 121 users learned something new — effectiveness proven across 8 countries
Zero marketing budget — organic reach proves the product itself drives engagement

originality.q: Your project is described as For Kids, By Kids. Why does who built it matter?
originality.a: It matters for 3 measurable reasons. First, design accuracy: we capped videos at 2 minutes and gave badges every 5 correct answers because that is what we as 10-year-olds actually find motivating — not because a researcher said so. Second, reach: 121 users across 8 countries found our app organically, which suggests peer-created content spreads naturally through sharing. Third, inspiration: showing that 3 elementary students can build 12 videos, 240 quiz questions, and a 360-example AI classifier sends a direct signal to the 25 to 30 million K-12 students targeted by the April 2025 Executive Order that they too can create, not just consume, AI technology.
keys: Designed by actual K-5 students: 2-minute videos and badges every 5 correct reflect real student motivation
Organic reach to 8 countries with 0 paid marketing — peer content spreads naturally
3 elementary students built 12 videos, 240 questions, 360-example classifier — inspires 25–30M peers

originality.q: Tell me about the most creative decision your team made while building this project.
originality.a: Our most creative decision was building our own AI training tool from scratch after 3 commercial platforms failed. Liner AI, Machine Learning for Kids, and Base44 all had critical limitations. Rather than giving up, our 3-person team used Claude to code a custom training interface with exactly 4 features we needed: category label definition, labeled example storage, a minimum 20-examples-per-category threshold, and a human-in-the-loop correction system. This transformed our project from a website with videos into a genuine AI engineering project. It gave us a live demo for our December science fair: 30 to 40 visitors typed real sentences into the classifier and watched it make decisions in real time. Building the tool ourselves also meant we trained on 360 examples with no usage limits.
keys: Built custom training tool after 3 platforms (Liner AI, ML for Kids, Base44) all failed
Custom tool: 4 specific features, 360-example capacity, no usage limits
Live science fair demo: 30–40 visitors tested real sentences on the classifier in real time

originality.q: How did you use storytelling and games to make a hard topic feel fun for a 10-year-old?
originality.a: We used 4 creativity strategies. First, we personified the AI problems — bias became the Unfair Robot, hallucination became the Make-It-Up Monster — giving each of our 4 topics a memorable face. Second, we used relatable examples: instead of explaining algorithmic bias, we said imagine a soccer team where the AI coach only picks players from 1 neighborhood. Third, we gamified the experience: the badge system rewards kids every 5 correct answers in a category from a pool of 60 questions, making it feel like leveling up. Fourth, we animated all 3 team members as on-screen teachers across 12 videos using Adobe Express — kids teaching kids. In testing, 90 percent of 121 users learned something new.
keys: 4 strategies: personified AI risks, relatable examples, badge every 5 correct, kid-animated videos
60-question pools per topic with badge every 5 correct — gamification with real depth
90% of 121 users learned something new — creativity produced measurable learning outcomes

originality.q: You built a custom classifier instead of using a pre-built tool. How does that make the project more original?
originality.a: Most student AI projects use Teachable Machine or Scratch — students plug in data and get a result without seeing how training actually works. We went 3 levels deeper. First, we designed the training interface itself using Claude. Second, our 3-person team wrote 360 labeled training examples — 30 per topic per person — and validated all 360 by hand. Third, we added a human-in-the-loop correction system that makes the classifier smarter with every user interaction beyond those initial 360 examples. That is the difference between using AI and building AI. It also produced a unique science fair demonstration: 30 to 40 visitors typed real chatbot sentences into our live tool and watched it classify them in real time.
keys: Built training interface, not just a model — coded with Claude, 4 engineering decisions made
360 hand-validated examples; feedback loop grows dataset beyond 360 with every user correction
Live demo at science fair: 30–40 visitors tested real sentences — unique to this project

originality.q: What would you add to the project if you had 6 more months?
originality.a: We have 3 specific plans. First, a Kid-Friendly Safety Bot: a chatbot fine-tuned on our existing 360 training examples that explains any AI safety question in language a 6-year-old understands, available 24/7 inside the app. Second, Classroom Demo Kits: we want to present at a minimum of 10 local elementary schools during weekly technology time, potentially reaching 250 to 300 additional students per school — that is 2,500 to 3,000 more students from classroom visits alone. Third, a larger training dataset: grow the classifier from 360 examples to 1,000 by adding 640 more examples across our 4 categories. All 3 plans build directly on what we proved works — a system that reached 121 users across 8 countries with 90 percent knowledge gain.
keys: Safety Bot: fine-tune on 360 existing examples — 24/7 AI safety help for 6-year-olds
10+ school demos × 250–300 students each = 2,500–3,000 additional students reached
Grow classifier from 360 to 1,000 examples (adding 640) to push toward classroom-grading accuracy

originality.q: How does your project show that AI can be a force for good when used responsibly?
originality.a: Our project uses AI in 3 responsible ways that directly model the behavior we teach in 12 videos and 240 quiz questions. First, Claude helped us write all the code — but every output was reviewed and tested by our 3-person team before publishing. 0 lines of unreviewed code made it to the live site. Second, ChatGPT helped generate 360 synthetic training examples — but all 360 were validated by hand before use. Third, the classifier itself is a tool for good: it helps kids detect when AI is being harmful. AI helped us build a tool that teaches kids to spot AI problems. In testing, 90 percent of 121 users learned something new about AI safety because of a product that itself used AI responsibly at every step.
keys: 0 unreviewed AI output published — all code and content reviewed by the 3-person team
360 AI-generated examples fully hand-validated before training the classifier
90% of 121 users learned AI safety using a tool built with responsible AI at every step

presentation.q: Tell me about the 12 videos you made. How did you create them and what do they cover?
presentation.a: We made 12 animated videos — 3 per topic across our 4 AI safety categories: bias, hallucination, privacy, and bad habits. We used Adobe Express to animate all 3 team members as on-screen teachers, then uploaded all 12 to YouTube and embedded them in our website using iframes. Each video runs about 2 minutes. We kept them short because K-5 students typically lose focus after 2 to 3 minutes of video. Within each 3-video set, we structured a learning progression: video 1 introduces the concept, video 2 shows a real-world example, and video 3 teaches what to do about it. Animating ourselves as teachers was a deliberate choice — when kids see peers explaining something rather than adults, engagement goes up. The 12 videos are the entry point to our 3-step learning loop: watch, quiz, earn badge.
keys: 12 videos = 3 per topic × 4 topics; ~2 minutes each to match K-5 attention spans
3-video progression per topic: introduce concept, real-world example, what to do
Kids teaching kids: all 3 team members animated as on-screen teachers using Adobe Express

presentation.q: How did you present at the science fair and what happened when visitors tried your app?
presentation.a: At our December science fair we used a 3-part approach. First, a hook question: do you know what AI hallucination is? Almost nobody knew. That got attention in under 5 seconds. Second, a live app demo: visitors picked 1 of 4 topics, watched a 2-minute video, answered 5 quiz questions from our 60-question pool, and earned a badge. Third, a live classifier demo: visitors typed their own real chatbot sentences and watched the 360-example classifier predict which of 4 problem types it was. In total, 30 to 40 people tested the app. Their feedback led to 3 improvements: we shortened long quiz questions, increased button sizes for phones, and the most critical fix — randomized the correct answer position across all 240 questions because multiple visitors spotted the option 1 bug.
keys: 30–40 visitors at December science fair tested both the app and live classifier
Hook: 'Do you know what AI hallucination is?' — almost nobody did — attention in under 5 seconds
3 improvements from fair: shorter questions, bigger buttons, randomized all 240 answer positions

presentation.q: Why did you choose video as part of your learning design and how does it connect to your quiz?
presentation.a: We chose video for 3 reasons grounded in our Phase 1 interviews. First, K-5 kids are still developing reading stamina — a 3-paragraph explanation of hallucination would lose most of our audience in under 30 seconds. A 2-minute video holds attention far better. Second, parents in our October-November interviews told us directly that their kids will not sit and read a webpage about AI safety. Third, video lets us show concepts visually — like animating an AI confidently stating a made-up fact. The video connects to the quiz through a 3-step loop: watch the 2-minute video, answer up to 60 quiz questions, earn a badge every 5 correct answers. Our testing confirmed this works: 90 percent of 121 users gained new knowledge.
keys: Video chosen because Phase 1 parent interviews confirmed kids won't read a webpage
3-step loop: 2-minute video → 60-question pool → badge every 5 correct answers
90% of 121 users gained new knowledge — validated the video-to-quiz learning sequence

presentation.q: Describe what your app looks like to a judge who has never seen it before.
presentation.a: The home screen has 4 large colored tiles — 1 per AI safety topic. Each has an emoji and a 1-sentence description, like Hallucination: When AI makes things up. Tap a tile and a 2-minute animated video of our 3-person team plays. After the video, a quiz pulls 1 question at a time from a pool of 60 per topic, with a progress bar filling up as you go. Every 5 correct answers in a row, a Glitch Buster badge appears on your profile. There is also an AI Model Trainer section where you type any sentence and the classifier — trained on 360 examples — predicts which of the 4 problem types it shows. The design uses large buttons and high contrast text, tested on 4 device types: Chromebooks, Windows laptops, iPhones, and Android phones.
keys: 4 color-coded topic tiles → 2-minute video → 60-question pool → badge every 5 correct
AI Model Trainer: type any sentence, 360-example classifier predicts the AI problem type
Tested on 4 device types — 12 videos, 240 questions, 1 live classifier tool

presentation.q: You reached 121 users across 8 countries. How did you promote your project?
presentation.a: We promoted through 3 channels, all at zero cost. First, our December science fair: 30 to 40 visitors tested the app in person and many shared it afterward. Second, we wrote letters to our teacher and principal asking permission to present to our classmates — giving us a direct K-5 school audience. Third, we created a Google Form survey and asked parents to share it on social media. Our GitHub Pages hosting was free. Despite no paid promotion, we reached 121 users in 8 countries with 715 total interactions — an average of 5.9 interactions per user. Our stated goal in Project 20260094 is to reach hundreds more students by our December 5, 2026 milestone through ongoing classroom demos at local elementary schools.
keys: 3 free promotion channels: science fair (30–40 visitors), classroom letters, parent social media sharing
121 users in 8 countries and 715 interactions — zero paid marketing, zero dollars spent
Goal: hundreds more students by December 5, 2026 via classroom demos at local elementary schools

presentation.q: How do the videos, quiz, and classifier work together as a complete learning experience?
presentation.a: The 3 components each play a different learning role and build on each other deliberately. The 12 videos — 3 per topic, each about 2 minutes — introduce concepts visually: you see an AI making up a fact, not just a definition of hallucination. The 240-question quiz — 60 per topic — reinforces concepts through repeated practice, with a badge every 5 correct answers keeping motivation high. The custom classifier — trained on 360 examples — brings the experience into the real world: you type a sentence from a real chatbot and see the AI decide what type of problem it is. The loop is watch and learn, quiz and remember, test and apply. Our data shows this 3-part approach worked: 90 percent of 121 users reported gaining new knowledge in just 1 session, and 5.9 interactions per user shows they explored multiple topics.
keys: 3-component loop: 12 videos (learn) → 240 questions (practice) → 360-example classifier (apply)
Badge every 5 correct across 60-question pools — sustains motivation throughout
90% knowledge gain; 5.9 interactions per user — both confirm the 3-part loop works

presentation.q: What feedback did you receive about your videos specifically and what did you change?
presentation.a: We received video feedback through 3 channels: internal team review, our December science fair with 30 to 40 visitors, and the Google Form reaching 121 users across 8 countries with 10 written responses. The most common feedback was that explanations felt too fast for younger kids in grades K through 2. We slowed pacing in 3 of the 12 videos and added pause time between key points. Science fair visitors told us video thumbnails were not clearly numbered, so kids did not know which of the 3 videos per topic to watch first. We numbered them Video 1, 2, and 3 and added a 1-sentence description under each. Our form data also showed that users who watched a video before taking the quiz had higher knowledge gain than users who skipped the video.
keys: Pacing fixed in 3 of 12 videos based on feedback from K-2 users at science fair
Added numbered sequence (Video 1, 2, 3) and 1-sentence descriptions — usability fix from 30–40 visitors
Form data proved video-first users had higher knowledge gain than quiz-only users across 121 total users
